80: **England** (W2 D1, +4 GD) face **Congo DR** (W1 D1 L1, +1 GD) at 9:00 AM PT in Atlanta. England are without RB Reece James (hamstring) and CB Jarell Quansah (ankle), with Djed Spence the likeliest replacement. Deputy RB Tino Livramento was lost before the tournament to a calf injury. Kane and Bellingham have scored in 10 of England's last 11 tournament wins. Kane had just 10 first-half touches vs Panama — fewer than GK Pickford — but headed in Bellingham's cross for the winner. Congo DR held Portugal 1-1 in the group stage using a disciplined low block, beat Uzbekistan 3-1, and lost only to Colombia 1-0. The winner faces Mexico at the Azteca on July 5, where Mexico have won all 4 home matches without conceding.
82: **Belgium**, level with one other team on 5 points with their position in the group not yet separated, take on **Senegal** at 1:00 PM PT — a **Senegal** side that finished 3rd in **Group I** on 3 points but carries a goal difference of +2 and the physicality to trouble any opponent. **Belgium**'s failure to fully separate themselves in the standings hints at inconsistency that **Senegal** will look to exploit. This is a tie where structure and set-piece threat could matter every bit as much as individual quality.
81: **USA** (W2 L1, +4 GD) host **Bosnia and Herzegovina** at 5:00 PM PT in Santa Clara. The Americans haven't won a knockout match since 2002 and have lost their last 10 games against European opposition. Pulisic is expected to start after playing just 77 minutes across the group stage due to a calf injury. All yellow cards are wiped — Adams, McKennie, Richards, and Balogun return. Bosnia had the fewest entries into the opponent's box of any team to reach the R32 and are expected to sit in a mid-block. They beat Wales and Italy on penalties in qualifying and their only group win was against Qatar.
78: **Côte d'Ivoire**, level with one other team on 6 points with their position not yet separated, open the day at 10:00 AM PT against **Norway**, who sit 2nd in **Group I** on the same 6 points but with a goal difference of +1 to **Côte d'Ivoire**'s +2. The two sides arrive as the closest-matched pairing of this round, divided by the finest of group-stage margins, and that razor-thin gap promises a knockout tie with intensity from the very first whistle. Neither team has shown any inclination to sit deep — expect an open, high-tempo contest where the next goal could decide everything.
77: **France**, the runaway leaders of **Group I** on a flawless 9 points and a goal difference of +8, meet **Sweden** at 2:00 PM PT — a **Sweden** side that finished 3rd in **Group F** on 4 points and must produce something close to a perfect performance against the tournament's most dominant group-stage outfit. **France**'s attacking depth and defensive solidity have made them look near-unplayable so far; **Sweden** must disrupt their rhythm early and turn this into a battle of attrition if they are to pull off one of the round's biggest upsets.
79: **Mexico**, perfect through the group stage as leaders of **Group A** on 9 points and a goal difference of +6, close the evening at 6:00 PM PT against **Ecuador**, who advanced as 3rd in **Group E** on 4 points with a goal difference of +0. The gap in group-stage output is real, but **Ecuador**'s resilience and set-piece threat make them genuinely dangerous opponents regardless of form lines. Playing in front of a home crowd that will lift the roof, **Mexico** are clear favourites — though **Ecuador** arrive with every reason to believe they can spring a surprise.
